moveAnnotations.annotation: We're opening with the queen's pawn, staking our claim to the central squares and preparing to build a classical pawn center. This move immediately puts pressure on Black's e5 and c5 squares while freeing our dark-squared bishop for development. The d4 opening leads to more positional, strategic games compared to 1.e4, and we're signaling our intention to control space and gradually build our advantage. Our plan will typically involve supporting this central pawn with moves like Nf3, c4, and potentially e4 later to create a powerful pawn duo.

Black immediately challenges our central pawn with the Old Benoni Defense, one of the most aggressive responses to 1.d4. This move creates immediate tension in the center and signals Black's intention to fight for space rather than allow us a comfortable classical setup. While this looks aggressive, it actually weakens Black's d6 square and gives us the option to advance with d5, gaining space and potentially creating a bind on the dark squares. Black is essentially saying 'I'm not going to let you have the center without a fight' and is willing to accept some positional compromises for active counterplay.

We're advancing our pawn to gain space and create the characteristic pawn structure of the Benoni Defense. This move gives us a significant space advantage on the kingside and restricts Black's pieces, particularly making it harder for Black to develop the knight to its natural c6 square. Our pawn on d5 also controls the important c6 and e6 squares, potentially forcing Black's pieces into more passive positions. The downside is that we've somewhat fixed the pawn structure early, but in return we get excellent chances to build a kingside attack and maintain long-term pressure.

Black solidifies the position by supporting the c5 pawn and preparing to develop pieces in a more restrained setup. This move is almost forced, as the c5 pawn was under attack and needed immediate support to maintain Black's foothold in the center. However, this does create a somewhat cramped position for Black, with limited space for piece development. Black's plan will typically involve moves like g6, Bg7, Nf6, and eventually trying to create counterplay on the queenside or break with ...f5 or ...e6 at the right moment.

We're developing our knight to its most natural square, where it supports our space-gaining d5 pawn and prepares for further central expansion with e4. This move also opens up possibilities for our light-squared bishop to develop to c4 or e2, and keeps our options flexible for kingside development. The knight on c3 can later support advances like e4-e5 or help in a kingside attack, while also being well-placed to support our central pawn chain. We're following classical development principles while maintaining the pressure created by our space advantage.

Black begins the characteristic kingside fianchetto setup that defines the Schmid Variation of the Old Benoni, preparing to develop the bishop to g7 where it will become a powerful long-range piece. This move signals Black's intention to castle kingside quickly and create a solid defensive structure, while the future bishop on g7 will exert pressure along the long diagonal toward our queenside. The fianchetto setup also prepares potential counterplay with moves like ...f5 later in the game, trying to challenge our central dominance. While this development is somewhat slow, it creates a robust defensive setup that can withstand our space advantage and potentially launch counterattacks when the time is right.
